request.question: NFL Street 3 is the third installment of the "NFL Street" series, which  American football wide receiver, was an official spokesperson of the game?

title: NFL Street 2
Braylon Edwards
Johnnie Morant
NFL Tour
Bumpin' My Music
NFL Street 3
J. D. Hill
Chad Johnson
NFL Street
Kenny Stafford
sentences: NFL Street 2 is American football video game developed by EA Sports BIG and published by Electronic Arts.
It was originally released for the PlayStation 2, GameCube and Xbox on December 22, 2004.
It features then-New York Giants tight end Jeremy Shockey and rapper Xzibit on the cover.
The game is compatible with "Madden NFL 2006", as players can import their "Own The City" players to it.
The game's compatibility also extends to other EA games like "", "SSX 3", "Madden NFL 2005", "NCAA Football 2005", "NFL Street", "", "NBA Live 2005", and "NBA Street Vol.
2", where if players have these games on their memory cards, they can earn 25,000 points in the game.
Braylon Jamel Edwards (born February 21, 1983) is a former American football wide receiver in the National Football League (NFL).
He played college football for the University of Michigan, earned unanimous All-American honors, and was recognized as the top college wide receiver.
He was also the first receiver in Big Ten Conference history to record three consecutive 1,000-yard seasons and only the third to do so in NCAA Division I-A.
He was selected by the Cleveland Browns with the third overall pick in the 2005 NFL Draft.
Johnnie Morant, Jr. (born December 7, 1981) is a former gridiron football wide receiver.
He was drafted by the Oakland Raiders in the fifth round of the 2004 NFL Draft.
He played college football at Syracuse.
He is currently the Head Strength and Conditioning Coach and Wide Receivers coach at East Carter High School.
While at Kentucky Christian University, Coach Morant help mentor an All-American Wide Receiver who was invited to an All Star game and an All Mid-South Academic Wide Receiver.
NFL Tour is an arcade football video game.
It was released on January 8, 2008.
It was developed under the EA Sports BIG division of EA Sports, which is also responsible for such titles as "SSX", "NBA Street", "NFL Street", and the "FIFA Street" games.
The game could be considered a successor to the "NFL Street" franchise, but it was converted to the new hardware (PlayStation 3 / Xbox 360).
Trey Wingo from ESPN is the game's voice announcer.
"Bumpin' My Music" is a 2006 single by Ray Cash as his third single from "C.O.D. (Cash on Delivery)".
This song is Cash's first single to reach any chart in "Billboard" magazine or to be featured on a countdown.
/ /chart .
The track is the third song on the album with a running time of 4:01.
Scarface, who is an idol of Ray Cash is featured on the song.
For a period of time, it was featured on BET 106 & Park voting page.
It was also featured on the video game NFL Street 3.
NFL Street 3 is the third installment of the "NFL Street" series, released in November 2006 for the PlayStation 2 and PlayStation Portable consoles.
This installment featured more game modes and unlockable features than previous versions.
Chad Johnson of the Cincinnati Bengals appears on the cover and was the official spokesperson of the game.

J. D. Hill (born October 30, 1948, in Stockton, California) is a retired American football wide receiver who played seven seasons in the National Football League for the Buffalo Bills and Detroit Lions; he played college football at Arizona State University.
His son, Lonzell Hill, also played wide receiver in the NFL.
Another son, Shelby Hill, was a wide receiver for Syracuse University.
Chad Javon Johnson (born January 9, 1978), formerly Chad Ochocinco, is a former American football wide receiver.

He played college football for both Santa Monica College and Oregon State University, and played eleven seasons in the National Football League (NFL) for the Cincinnati Bengals, New England Patriots, and the Miami Dolphins.
He was drafted by the Cincinnati Bengals of the National Football League (NFL) in the second round of the 2001 NFL Draft, and played for the Bengals for 10 seasons.
In 2011, Johnson was traded to the New England Patriots which he played for in Super Bowl XLVI.
In 2012, Johnson played for the Miami Dolphins during preseason but was released following his arrest for domestic violence.

He played for the Montreal Alouettes of the Canadian Football League (CFL) from 2014 to 2015, and played one game in 2017 for Mexican team Monterrey Fundidores of the Liga de Fútbol Americano Profesional.
NFL Street is an American football video game developed by EA Tiburon and published by Electronic Arts.
It was originally released for the PlayStation 2, GameCube and Xbox on January 13, 2004.
Barry Sanders of the Detroit Lions, Shannon Sharpe of the Denver Broncos, and Ricky Williams of the Miami Dolphins grace the cover.
The game was followed by "NFL Street 2" and "NFL Street 3".
Kenny Stafford (born April 20, 1990) is a Canadian football wide receiver who is currently a member of the Edmonton Eskimos of the Canadian Football League (CFL).
In the United States he has been a member of the Atlanta Falcons (NFL), Pittsburgh Power (AFL) and Miami Dolphins (NFL).
Elsewhere in the CFL Stafford has been a member of the Calgary Stampeders, Montreal Alouettes and Winnipeg Blue Bombers.
He is the nephew of former American football wide receiver and 2013 Hall of Fame Inductee Cris Carter.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: NFL Street 3 is the third installment of the "NFL Street" series, which  American football wide receiver, was an official spokesperson of the game?
hop1_docs: NFL Street 3 | NFL Street 3 is the third installment of the "NFL Street" series, released in November 2006 for the PlayStation 2 and PlayStation Portable consoles.  This installment featured more game modes and unlockable features than previous versions.  Chad Johnson of the Cincinnati Bengals appears on the cover and was the official spokesperson of the game.

Tai Streets | Tai Lamar Streets (born April 20, 1977) is a former professional American football wide receiver in the National Football League (NFL).  He was selected with the second pick of the sixth round of the 1999 NFL Draft by the San Francisco 49ers.  He also played for the Detroit Lions in 2004.  He was the leading receiver for the national champion 1997 Michigan Wolverines football team.  Over the course of his career he was notable for fourth quarter performances in various bowl games and NFL playoff games.  As a professional athlete, he was known for his modesty.  As an amateur athlete, he was known as one of the best high school athletes in the city of Chicago.

NFL Street (series) | NFL Street is a series of sports video games developed by EA Tiburon and published by Electronic Arts.  It combines the talent and big names of the NFL with the atmosphere of street football.  It is much like the regular Madden NFL series, however plays are, in fact different.

Devin Street | Devin Curtis Street (born March 30, 1991) is an American football wide receiver who is currently a free agent.  He is in the National Football League (NFL).  He was drafted by the Dallas Cowboys in the fifth round of the 2014 NFL Draft.  He played college football at the University of Pittsburgh.

ESPN NFL 2K5 | ESPN NFL 2K5 is an American football video game developed by Visual Concepts and published by 2K Sports and the Sega Corporation.  It is the sixth installment of the "NFL 2K series".  The game was originally released on July 20, 2004, for both the PlayStation 2 and Xbox video game consoles.  It was the last "NFL 2K" game to be released before Electronic Arts signed an exclusivity deal with the NFL to make 2K's rival Madden NFL series the only officially licensed NFL game.  It was also the last game to be made by Sega before that happened.

NFL Tour | NFL Tour is an arcade football video game.  It was released on January 8, 2008.  It was developed under the EA Sports BIG division of EA Sports, which is also responsible for such titles as "SSX", "NBA Street", "NFL Street", and the "FIFA Street" games.  The game could be considered a successor to the "NFL Street" franchise, but it was converted to the new hardware (PlayStation 3 / Xbox 360).  Trey Wingo from ESPN is the game's voice announcer.

NFL Street | NFL Street is an American football video game developed by EA Tiburon and published by Electronic Arts.  It was originally released for the PlayStation 2, GameCube and Xbox on January 13, 2004.  Barry Sanders of the Detroit Lions, Shannon Sharpe of the Denver Broncos, and Ricky Williams of the Miami Dolphins grace the cover.  The game was followed by "NFL Street 2" and "NFL Street 3".

- Chad Johnson — [1]: "Chad Johnson of the Cincinnati Bengals appears on the cover"
- "was the official spokesperson of the game." — [1]: "was the official spokesperson of the game."

- Tai Lamar Streets — [2]: "Tai Lamar Streets (born April 20, 1977) is a former professional American football wide receiver"
- Devin Curtis Street — [4]: "Devin Curtis Street (born March 30, 1991) is an American football wide receiver"
- NFL Street (series) — [3]: "NFL Street is a series of sports video games"
- Barry Sanders — [7]: "Barry Sanders of the Detroit Lions"
- Shannon Sharpe — [7]: "Shannon Sharpe of the Denver Broncos"
- Ricky Williams — [7]: "Ricky Williams of the Miami Dolphins"
- Trey Wingo — [6]: "Trey Wingo from ESPN is the game's voice announcer."

Ambiguities:
- "Chad Johnson" — passages name him and his team but do not state his playing position; could refer to multiple name variants (e.g., "Chad Johnson" / "Chad Ochocinco").
- "Street" vs "Streets" — passage lists both "Tai Lamar Streets" and "Devin Curtis Street" (different surnames), which could cause confusion about which "Street(s)" is/are the wide receiver referenced.
- Multiple wide receivers appear in passages (Tai Lamar Streets, Devin Curtis Street), so without Chad Johnson's position explicitly given, the role "which American football wide receiver was an official spokesperson" is unresolved.

Bridge / missing fact:
- Missing relation: confirmation that "Chad Johnson" (the person named in [1]) is an American football wide receiver (his playing position is not stated in the provided passages).
- Also missing: any disambiguation of Chad Johnson's common name variants (e.g., "Chad Ochocinco") within these passages.

Retrieval direction:
- Open page "Chad Johnson (American football)" (or "Chad Ochocinco") to confirm his position and identity.
- Search query: "Chad Johnson Cincinnati Bengals wide receiver" (exact query) to retrieve authoritative profile.
- Search query: "Chad Ochocinco NFL wide receiver Wikipedia" to verify position and name variants.
- Page "NFL Street 3" (full article) to cross-check spokesperson credit if needed.

Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
response.hop2_query: Chad Johnson Cincinnati Bengals wide receiver
hop2_docs: Chad Johnson | Chad Javon Johnson (born January 9, 1978), formerly Chad Ochocinco, is a former American football wide receiver.  He played college football for both Santa Monica College and Oregon State University, and played eleven seasons in the National Football League (NFL) for the Cincinnati Bengals, New England Patriots, and the Miami Dolphins.  He was drafted by the Cincinnati Bengals of the National Football League (NFL) in the second round of the 2001 NFL Draft, and played for the Bengals for 10 seasons.  In 2011, Johnson was traded to the New England Patriots which he played for in Super Bowl XLVI.  In 2012, Johnson played for the Miami Dolphins during preseason but was released following his arrest for domestic violence.  He played for the Montreal Alouettes of the Canadian Football League (CFL) from 2014 to 2015, and played one game in 2017 for Mexican team Monterrey Fundidores of the Liga de Fútbol Americano Profesional.

Cobi Hamilton | Cobi Hamilton (born November 13, 1990) is an American football wide receiver for the Houston Texans of the National Football League (NFL).  He played college football at Arkansas.  He was considered one of the top wide receiver prospects for the 2013 NFL Draft.  He was selected by the Cincinnati Bengals with the 197th overall pick in the draft in the sixth round with a selection acquired from the New England Patriots in 2011 for Chad Johnson.

2011 Cincinnati Bengals season | The 2011 Cincinnati Bengals season was the franchise's 44th season as a professional football team and 42nd in the National Football League (NFL).  The Bengals entered the season coming off a 4–12 in 2010.  Head Coach Marvin Lewis was re-signed by the team.  Quarterback (QB) Carson Palmer demanded a trade and was dealt to the Oakland Raiders.  Wide receiver (WR) Chad Johnson was traded to the New England Patriots.  Replacing the two, the organization drafted QB Andy Dalton and WR AJ Green in the 2011 NFL Draft.  The start of the 2011 season was hindered by a lockout, which cancelled the teams' mini-camp.
Chad Johnson (disambiguation) | Chad Johnson (born 1978; formerly Chad Ochocinco) is an American football wide receiver.

1985 Cincinnati Bengals season | The 1985 Cincinnati Bengals season was their 16th in the National Football League (NFL).  Wide receiver Isaac Curtis, a premier Bengal for 12 years, retired shortly before training camp opened.  Second-year QB Boomer Esiason replaced Ken Anderson.  The Bengals set a club scoring record with 441 points.

Chris Henry (wide receiver) | Chris Henry (May 17, 1983 – December 17, 2009) was an American football wide receiver who played five seasons in the National Football League for the Cincinnati Bengals.  He played college football at West Virginia and was drafted by the Bengals in the third round of the 2005 NFL Draft.

NFL Street 3 | NFL Street 3 is the third installment of the "NFL Street" series, released in November 2006 for the PlayStation 2 and PlayStation Portable consoles.  This installment featured more game modes and unlockable features than previous versions.  Chad Johnson of the Cincinnati Bengals appears on the cover and was the official spokesperson of the game.
response.summary_2: NFL Street 3's official spokesperson was Chad Johnson, a former American football wide receiver.
